conversation_history.answer: No, I’m not working in the facility. I have a job lined up for when I get out.
conversation_history.question: Are you currently employed in a job here in the facility?

conversation_history.answer: Yes—I was working part-time at a local café before all this. About twenty hours a week, which helped cover daycare costs.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing that you have a job lined up for when you get out. Before entering this facility, were you working in a job?

conversation_history.answer: Um, yes. I’ll be a barista again at the same café—just full-time this time. I really enjoyed the customer work and it helps with daycare costs.
conversation_history.question: Can you tell me what kind of work you’re planning to do after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: I’m aiming for about 35 to 40 hours a week.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing that you’ll return as a full-time barista. About how many hours per week are you planning to work after your release?

conversation_history.answer: I guess I’d like to prove I’m reliable—show up on time, pick up extra shifts—and save enough for stable daycare. I also hope to build routine and maybe learn more skills, like supervising or barista training, so I can grow there.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing that. What goals or hopes do you have for your barista work after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: Um, I haven’t set anything in stone yet. I’d like to look into evening or online classes—maybe in business or hospitality management—once I’m settled back into work and daycare routines.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing your employment goals. Now let's talk about school plans. Do you plan to go to school or take classes after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: Um… I’m thinking maybe around 6 to 8 hours a week, depending on how things settle.
conversation_history.question: About how many hours each week do you think you would spend in evening or online classes after your release?

conversation_history.answer: Well, I’d like to build a solid foundation in business basics—budgeting, customer service, managing a small team. I hope that earning a certificate will open doors to supervisory roles down the line. Mostly, I want the confidence that I’m setting a good example for my kids and securing a stable future.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing your hours. What goals or hopes do you have for your education once you start those business or hospitality management classes?

conversation_history.answer: I finished high school and earned my diploma. I haven’t done any college yet.
conversation_history.question: Thank you for sharing your goals. What is the highest level of school you have finished—such as high school, GED, or college?

conversation_history.answer: No, I don’t have any formal degrees or certificates beyond my diploma. I did complete a short customer service workshop a couple of years ago, but nothing official like a certificate program.
conversation_history.question: Do you have any degrees, certificates, or special training beyond your high school diploma?

conversation_history.answer: Um, not yet. I haven’t enrolled in anything here so far, but I’m planning to sign up for the business basics course as soon as it’s available.
conversation_history.question: Are you currently enrolled in any classes or training programs here in the facility?

conversation_history.answer: Um…I’m hoping to support myself with the new job, but I may need to apply for temporary assistance—like SNAP—until I get fully settled.
conversation_history.question: Do you plan to apply for any financial assistance after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: I’d really like to build up a small emergency savings—enough for a couple months’ rent. I want to pay my own bills, support my kids, and eventually be debt-free. And hopefully even start saving for something like a car down payment.
conversation_history.question: What goals or hopes do you have for your finances after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: I’m currently single. I was with my kids’ dad, but we’re no longer together.
conversation_history.question: Do you have a partner, spouse, or someone you’re in a long-term relationship with?

conversation_history.answer: Yes, I’d say so. My mom’s been my rock—visiting me, helping me plan—and my dad’s been supportive, too. I feel lucky to have them on my side.
conversation_history.question: Thank you for sharing that. Do you have a strong, positive relationship with your parents?

conversation_history.answer: Um, yes—I’m close with my two younger sisters. They’ve been checking in a lot, sending letters and small care packages. My grandmother also writes me every week. It really helps.
conversation_history.question: Thank you for sharing about your parents. Do you have strong, positive relationships with your other family members?

conversation_history.answer: I was living in a small two-bedroom apartment near my parents’ house, with my two kids.
conversation_history.question: Can you tell me where you were living before entering this facility?

conversation_history.answer: I’m hoping to move back into my old apartment—my lease hasn’t expired yet, I think. If not, my mom’s offered that I can stay with her until I sort something permanent out.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing your previous home. Do you have a place lined up to stay right after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: I’m hoping to stay in my old apartment long-term—it’s close to my kids’ school and my new job. If that lease can’t be reinstated, my mom said I can stay with her temporarily while I find another place nearby.
conversation_history.question: Can you tell me where you plan to live long-term after your release, and will that be the same apartment or somewhere else?

conversation_history.answer: I really hope to have a stable, kid-friendly home where my kids feel safe and I can keep a steady routine. Ideally it’s close to their school and my new job so I can be there for them every day.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing your long-term plan. What goals or hopes do you have for your housing situation after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: I used to love reading novels—mysteries mostly—and knitting scarves for the kids. I also enjoyed taking walks in the park with them on weekends and trying out a new yoga class whenever I could.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing your housing goals. Before coming here, what hobbies or fun activities did you enjoy?

conversation_history.answer: I’ve been reading some paperback novels from the small library here—mostly mysteries still. I’ve also picked up knitting again, working on a scarf. And I try to join the light stretching group a couple times a week, even though space is tight.
conversation_history.question: Thank you for sharing those hobbies you enjoyed before. What activities or hobbies do you enjoy while you’re living here in the facility?

conversation_history.answer: I’d love to join a local book club to keep me reading and meeting new people. I also want to get back into a weekly yoga class—and maybe start a weekend knitting circle at the community center so I can share skills and make friends. And of course, more walks in the park with the kids.
conversation_history.question: Thank you for sharing what you’ve been doing here. Are there any hobbies or activities you’d like to pursue after you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: Yes—I plan to spend time with my sister, who’s been my rock through all this, and my good friend Carla, who’s also into yoga. And of course I can’t wait to have regular park days with the kids.
conversation_history.question: Do you have any close friends or companions you plan to spend time with after your release?

conversation_history.answer: I’m picturing a pretty balanced routine—morning yoga with Carla and maybe new friends from the studio, afternoons at the park with the kids (and playdates with other parents), weekly book club meetings—and Sunday dinners at my sister’s. I’d like to keep that mix of family time, creative outlets like knitting circle, and a small but supportive friend group.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing your companions. How do you imagine your social life will look once you’re released?

conversation_history.answer: I don’t have a history of drug use and only occasionally enjoy a glass of wine. I feel confident in managing that on my own, but if there’s a local support group or healthy-coping workshop, I’d be open to checking it out.
conversation_history.question: Thanks for sharing about your social life. After you’re released, will you need any support or resources to help with alcohol or drug use?
